title: Stratospheric ozone concentration projections
subtitle: Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).
caption:
Stratospheric ozone concentration projections

Stratospheric ozone concentrations with projections to 2100 based on chemistry-climate models. These are measured as global and regional averages relative to levels in 1960 (1960 = 0).

The line chart tracks stratospheric ozone concentration changes from 1960 through 2100, measuring global and regional averages relative to the 1960 baseline set at zero. The vertical axis spans from -140 to +40, representing the deviation from 1960 levels, while the horizontal axis covers the 140-year period. Three distinct trajectories appear: the Arctic region, the Global average, and the Antarctic region. The Arctic line begins near zero in 1960, dips slightly below the baseline until the mid-1980s, then rises steadily, crossing above zero around 2005 and reaching approximately +35 by 2100. The Global average follows a similar but more moderate path, declining to roughly -15 around 2000 before recovering to just above zero by 2100. The Antarctic line shows the most extreme movement, plunging from zero in 1960 to a minimum near -120 around 2005, then climbing back toward -5 by 2100 without fully recovering to baseline levels. All three lines converge near zero at the start, diverge significantly through the late twentieth century, and show recovery trends in the twenty-first century, though at different rates and magnitudes. The figures represent the mean across a number of model runs, and model projections carry notable uncertainty around such average trends. The Arctic region displays the strongest recovery, exceeding its 1960 baseline by the largest margin, while the Antarctic region remains the furthest below baseline throughout the entire period and ends 2100 still substantially depleted relative to 1960 levels.
